blocks.content: Ethan was trying to decide whether the bench needed shadow when the bell above the café door rang.
That was an act of discipline.
A mature, grounded, emotionally stable decision.
Then Mr. Alvarez dropped a spoon.

Cassandra stood just inside The Copper Mug, one hand still on the door, sunlight catching in her red hair like it had followed her inside. She looked around the café with open curiosity, taking in the mismatched chairs, the shelves of old books no one ever borrowed, the hanging plants Mr. Alvarez insisted were thriving even though two of them looked emotionally exhausted.
Not the quick smile from across the street.
Not the amused smile from the park.
Almost like she had been hoping he would be there and did not want to admit it too loudly.
Ethan forgot how to sit normally.
His elbow slipped slightly off the table.
The pencil rolled from his fingers and landed on the floor.
Mr. Alvarez appeared instantly, because apparently betrayal wore an apron.
Her eyes flicked toward Ethan, who was now bending down to retrieve his pencil with all the grace of a collapsing bookshelf.
Mr. Alvarez looked from Cassandra to Ethan, then back to Cassandra.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: Only because the regulars do not enter with that much light. It makes them nervous.
Ethan hit his head lightly on the edge of the table while trying to sit back up.
He closed his eyes for one tragic second.
Cassandra's smile widened, though she tried to hide it behind concern.
Mr. Alvarez set a menu on Ethan's table.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: This table has excellent lighting and one nervous artist.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: Both are included at no extra charge.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: That sounds like a good deal.
Ethan stared at the older man with the quiet desperation of someone being abandoned in public.
Mr. Alvarez leaned slightly toward him.
Cassandra stood beside the table for half a second, holding back a smile.
Ethan looked at the empty chair across from him.
But somehow it felt like it had been waiting.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: No. I mean—yes. No. You can sit. It's not taken.
She laughed softly and sat down.
But the air felt different with her sitting there, across from his sketchbook, across from his coffee, across from the part of himself he usually kept safely folded away.
Cassandra looked at the page before he could close it.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Did you make it less sad?
That way she had of saying something light and leaving it heavy.
Cassandra reached for the menu and opened it upside down.
For one second, they both looked at it.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I'm testing the café's design accessibility.
Not a nervous sound this time.
Cassandra smiled like she had been waiting for that too.
It felt like it had been waiting.
